title: Should investors stop using the Mag-7 as a Source of Funds?

Happy Wednesday out there team 42, it's
your skipper here Darius Delta
presenting our macro minute for
Wednesday, July 8th, 2026. As always,
we'll start with the executive summary
from today's latest morning note. So,
let's dive right in. Today's key macro
question is, should investors stop using
the Mac 7 as a source of funds? The
short answer is, over the long term, no,
absolutely not. This theme should reward
investors for years. Over the short to
medium term, however, the rising
probability of a risk-off market regime
will cause systematic investors and
pseudo systematic investors to reduce
their gross exposure, which implies
booking gains in recent winners and
covering shorts in this under-loved
equity factor. Towards the analytical
nuance during the NATO summit in Turkey,
the US revoked Iran's oil waiver and
struck 80-plus targets. President Trump
called the ceasefire over and said it
was a waste of time following US strikes
over the Strait of Hormuz attacks.
Investors had viewed June's memorandum
as a lasting de-escalation, so its
complacency now looks tenuous.
Elsewhere, the June New York Fed survey
of consumer expectations data did not
support the consensus outlook for
peaking inflation. The certain inflation
and labor indicators in the in the
survey supported our sticky inflation
theme.
Costlier energy from a resumption of the
Strait of Hormuz crisis lifts
inflationary expectations, increasing
scrutiny around today's Fed minutes,
which is the first release under new Fed
chair Kevin Warsh. Chair Warsh's first
press conference was the most hawkish on
record according to Bloomberg's natural
language processing and data back to
2011. This afternoon's Fed minutes may
send a similar hawkish vibe. Overnight,
South Korea's Kospi plunged 5% into a
bear market. The year-to-date's top
equity benchmark faces volatility as
investors rotate from semiconductors
towards stocks with cheaper valuations.
This rotation out of the picks and
shovels may favor Mac 7 constituents on
a relative basis, many of which have
been left for dead on in in recent
months as our high perception source of
funds theme became consensus as most of
our themes tend to do on a two to four
quarter lag to their introduction.
Nvidia currently sits
at its cheapest valuation since before
the AI boom, shedding $1 trillion in
under 2 months, Nvidia now trades at 18
times forward earnings. Such lows last
appeared in early 2019. Nvidia now
trades below the S&P 500 index and the
Nasdaq 100 index at 20 times forward
earnings and nearly 23 times forward
earnings respectively. Despite investors
expecting the fourth fastest revenue
growth among S&P 500 constituents,
Nvidia's price to next 12-month earnings
ratio now sits below half of the other
stocks in the index, including much
older, slower-growing companies like
Hershey's and Dominion Energy. While
investors have appropriately used Nvidia
as a source of funds to capitalize AI
adopters on a trend basis, they should
keep in mind that, quote, roots don't
grow to the Earth's core, just as,
quote, trees don't grow to the moon.
Investors should expect moderation
and/or outright reversal of these source
of funds flows if the Strait of Hormuz
crisis deepens from here. We'll
obviously think that is transitory based
on the short-term answer to today's key
macro question. So, as always, we'll
wrap up with the question from our
community. This one's titled gold
outlook. Says, curious on folks' takes
on gold here. Says, longer-term, the key
42 macro thesis on the supply-demand
imbalance in the Treasury market is not
changing. Hence, gold is likely in a
long-term bull cycle, correct? We had a
parabolic rally that has since
corrected. What do you think needs to
occur for gold to find a local low? I'm
wondering if stocks go through a late
summer correction, how would that impact
gold? So, if the late summer correction
is driven by
a resurgence of the Strait of Hormuz
crisis, it's going to have a negative
impact on gold, much like what we saw
throughout, you know, March. And so,
ultimately,
uh this yeah, this Dr. Bull signal for
gold here. And so, ultimately, investors
need to be be aware that
gold is a long-term structural uptrend,
but it's obviously being be failed by a
couple of critical market dynamics that,
you know, are kind of throwing a
transitory wrench in in this in the in
the in the trade. Number one, the advent
of Kevin Walsh as the new Fed chair,
dating back to his announcement in
January,
really shifted the dynamic as relates to
the longer-term outlook for US interest
rate policy and US balance sheet policy
in a way that was very unfavorable for
gold and and supportive of the dollar
and and long-term treasury bonds. We
ultimately think that is a uh
that will be proven to be an incorrect
interpretation, but we're what we're at
least a couple of quarters away from
investors starting to get clues on on on
that conclusion. It's our belief that
the net result of the five task forces
that Kevin Chair Wash outlined at the
June FOMC meeting will be dovish and
perhaps materially dovish on a net
basis, not all dovish but materially
dovish on a net basis. And if that's
true, then you have to expect a
resumption of the rally from a lower
high uh starting from a higher low
rather in this in in bullion in the
coming in the coming months. Uh and and
then secondarily, uh the Strait of
Hormuz crisis we talked about is not a
energy supply is not just an energy
supply shock supply shock. It is also a
liquidity crisis as well because the the
net international investment surplus
economies that are operating in the
Strait of Hormuz and who receive
meaningful percentage of their energy
supplies from the Strait of Hormuz,
they're not able to you know, generate
the household savings corporate profits
that they need to recycle back into the
back into the treasury market and
ultimately uh and just back into global
capital markets. And so ultimately you
see a mass reduction in liquidity that
causes these central banks which have
accumulated a lot of gold in recent
years in in lieu of US dollars, it
causes those central banks to liquidate
their gold exposures to prevent balance
of payments crises in their own
economies. And so that dynamic can pick
up again in in the coming months if if
the the the sort of seesaw dynamic is
is quote unquote over and a waste of
time. So we'll wrap it up there. Dairy
still here presenting our our macro
minute for Wednesday, July 8th, 2026.
